coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: A facile and green fluorescence probe for free chlorine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Mingyue
Zhang, Liangliang
Zou, Mengbing
Lan, Chuanqing
Zhan, Zhihua
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) emitted strong blue fluorescence in aqueous solution, and it was found that the free chlorine could efficiently quench the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs, which was utilized as a sensitive, low-cost and green fluorescence probe for the detection of free chlorine. Under optimal conditions, a linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs and the concentration of free chlorine in the range from 0.01 to 100μM was obtained with a detection limit of 5nM (3σ). The proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of free residual chlorine in tap water and swimming pool with quantitative recovery (91–106%). The developed N,S-CDs fluorescence probe provides a new and promising tool for the water quality monitoring in the environmental analysis fields with low cost and excellent performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Free chlorine
Nitrogen
Sulfur

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism for the fluorescence response of N,S-CDs to free chlorine
Factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of free chlorine
Analytical performances for free chlorine sensing
Specificity of the fluorescent probe for free chlorine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of the N,S-CDs (a); high resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), and S2p (d) peaks of the N,S-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of the N,S-CDs (a), CA (b) and l-cysteine (c). Inset: photograph of the N,S-CDs solution under irradiation of natural light (right) and UV light (left). (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs (a), N,S-CDs+Cl (b) and fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs (c), N,S-CDs+Cl (d). Inset: photograph of the N,S-CDs solution in the absence (left) and presence (right) of 100μM free chlorine under irradiation of UV light (λ =365nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS spectra of the N,S-CDs after the addition of free chlorine.
XPS spectra of the N,S-CDs after the addition of free chlorine. The high resolution XPS of N1s (a) and S2p (b).
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of 100μM free chlorine.

The fluorescence response of N,S-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of free chlorine in a pH 5 solution (from top: 0, 0.01, 0.04, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4,0.8, 1, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100μM). Inset shows the linear response range of N,S-CDs to free chlorine.
Comparison of fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs after the addition of different ions in a pH 5 solution. Concentrations of heavy metal ions were 0.1mM, concentrations of other ions were 1mM.
Comparison of LOD of different detection schemes for free chlorine assays.
Detection results of free chlorine in water.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: A facile and green fluorescence probe for free chlorine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources of Education Ministry, Guangxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guilin Normal College, Guilin 541001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) emitted strong blue fluorescence in aqueous solution, and it was found that the free chlorine could efficiently quench the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs, which was utilized as a sensitive, low-cost and green fluorescence probe for the detection of free chlorine. Under optimal conditions, a linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs and the concentration of free chlorine in the range from 0.01 to 100μM was obtained with a detection limit of 5nM (3σ). The proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of free residual chlorine in tap water and swimming pool with quantitative recovery (91–106%). The developed N,S-CDs fluorescence probe provides a new and promising tool for the water quality monitoring in the environmental analysis fields with low cost and excellent performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlorine is a powerful oxidizing agent and has been extensively used in water disinfection, blanching and numerous manufacturing processes [1]. In water treatment, the concentration of free residual chlorine, the sum of dissolved chlorine (Cl2), hypochlorous acid (HClO) and hypochlorite (ClO−) must be strictly controlled, namely neither too low nor too high. Free residual chlorine with too low level cannot kill viruses and pathogenic bacteria in water. However, free residual chlorine with too high level may also be harmful, because the excess free chlorine would react with organic compounds existing in the water to produce a large number of undesirable compounds, especially trihalomethanes (THMs) [2,3], which have been reported to be potentially harmful to human and animals [4–6]. Therefore, the detection and control of free residual chlorine concentration in drinking water, swimming pool water and wastewater for reuse are extremely necessary. By now, many analytical methods based on different principles including colorimetric method [7], chemiluminescence methods [8,9], electro-chemical methods [10,11], liquid chromatographic methods [12,13] and fluorescence methods [14] have been developed for the determination of free chlorine. Although each method has its advantages, many reported techniques involve still poor selectivity, low sensitivity, tedious and time-consuming procedures. Thus, the design of convenient and inexpensive approaches for the sensitive and selective detection of free residual chlorine with rapid, lower-cost and easy manipulation is in ever increasing demand.

The carbon-based dots (CDs), a new family member of carbon nanomaterials, are generally composed of SP2 hybridized carbon atoms with abundant oxygenous and hydrogenous residues with the sizes below 10nm [15,16]. Owing to outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, resistance to environmental change and robust chemical inertness, CDs are attracting considerable attention in bioimaging [17,18], photocatalysis [19,20], and light-emitting devices [21]. Various methods have been reported in preparation of fluorescent CDs, such as chemical oxidation methods [22,23], electrochemical oxidation processes [24,25], carbonizing organics routes [26–28] and hydrothermal cutting strategies [29,30]. Nevertheless, most of these methods are unsatisfactory owing to relatively low fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY, usually less than 50%), expensive equipment required or complicated procedures. Thus, there is a great need to develop facile, low-cost and high-yield method for the preparation of CDs with strong fluorescence emission.

Recently, Dong et al. used citric acid (CA) and l-cysteine to prepare nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) through a one-step hydrothermal treatment. Compared with other reported CDs, the N,S-CDs exhibit very high FLQY (73%) and excitation-independent emission, resulting from the synergy effect of the doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms [31]. In this work, we found that the strong oxidative free chlorine can destroy the surface passivation layer of the N,S-CDs, and leading to significant fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs. Thus, a sensitive, rapid, facile, low-cost and green fluorescent probe was developed for the determination of free chlorine in water based on the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs. To the best of our knowledge, the proposed N,S-CDs fluorescent probe for free chlorine is one of the most sensitive probe.

All fluorescence spectra were obtained on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). The emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 400–600nm upon excitation at 345nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were characterized by a Cary UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100F Field Emission Transmission electron microscope (Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on a ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (Thermo Elemental). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed with a D/max-2500V/PC powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra were obtained on a PE Spectrum One Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed by a FL3-P-TCSPC time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Jvon). Raman spectra were obtained on a Raman Microscope (Renishaw, UK). Citric acid (CA, 99.9%) and l-cysteine (97.0%) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. A 10% sodium hypochlorite standardized by iodometric titration was used for daily preparation of diluted free chlorine working standards. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Water was purified with a Milli-Q plus 185 equip from Millipore (Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the work.

The N,S-CDs were prepared by thermal treatment of CA and l-cysteine according to a method that has been described elsewhere [31]. Briefly, 2g of CA (9.5mmol) and 1g of l-cysteine (8.3mmol) were dissolved in 5mL of ultrapure water, followed by evaporation at 70°C until dry within 12h. The resultant thick syrup was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave at 200°C for 3h with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant solution was adjusted to pH 7 with 1M NaOH, and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 30min to remove large precipitates. The as-prepared N,S-CDs were purified by dialysis (the cutoff of the dialysis membrane was equivalent to Mw≈1000). The purified N,S-CDs were dispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4°C for further use.

A local tap water sample was collected and used in this study, part of the water was boiled to obtain the boiling water samples, and two water samples from swimming pool were collected from adult pool and children pool in the natatorium of Guilin sports center, respectively. All detection experiments were performed under the same conditions. Aliquots (6μL) of tap water samples were spiked with standard sodium hypochlorite solutions (6μL; final concentration, 0–100μM). The spiked samples were then diluted to 120μL with phosphate buffer (0.1M, pH 5.0) containing N,S-CDs (final concentration, 1μg/mL). The mixture was stirred and equilibrated for 10min before the spectral measurements.

The N,S-CDs solution exhibits a long-term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation at room temperature. The TEM image (Fig. 1 ) shows that the size of the as-prepared N,S-CDs is distributed in the range from 4 to 9nm. XPS results (Fig. 2 ) indicate that the N,S-CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s exhibits three main peaks (Fig. 2b). The binding energy peak at 283.8eV confirms the graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of the N,S-CDs. The peak at about 284.8eV suggests the presence of CO, CS, and CN, and the peak around 287.7eV could be assigned to CO. The high-resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 2c) reveal the presence of both pyridinic type (399.7eV) and pyrrolic type (400.6eV) N atoms. The high-resolution spectra of S2p (Fig. 2d) reveal the presence of CSC units [32–34]. FTIR spectra (Fig. S1) confirm the presence of NH, SH, oxygen-containing groups (OH, COO−, CO, CO), CS and CN. Raman spectroscopy was used to confirm the quality of the N,S-CDs (Fig. S2). Two major features, D band and G band, were observed at around 1385 and 1575cm−1 respectively. Heteroatom doping has no obvious effect to the peak position of D and G bands [35,36]. The intensity ratio of the D and G band (I D/I G) is a measurement of the disorder extent, and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon. I D/I G for the N,S-CDs is about 0.5, indicating its high quality [25]. XRD was used to characterize the obtained N,S-CDs (Fig. S3). Like most reported CDs [26,37], the N,S-CDs show a broader (002) peak centered at about 25.5°, which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms [38] and confirms the graphene structure of the N,S-CDs.

The N,S-CDs in aqueous solution show yellow color (inset of Fig. 3 ) and have two typical UV–vis absorption peaks at 242 and 345nm, respectively. While the absorption of CA is above 230nm, and the absorption of l-cysteine is very weak below 230nm (Fig. 3). The peak at 242nm, corresponding to the П→П* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, leads to nearly no observed fluorescence signal [39]. The other transition centered about at 345nm due to the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states results in strong emission [40,41]. The emission wavelength of N,S-CDs is excitation independent. The maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength of the N,S-CDs are at 345 and 420nm, respectively (Fig. 3). The N,S-CDs exhibit an apparent UV–vis absorption band centered around 345nm, which corresponds well to the excitation spectra. Very bright blue luminescence under the illumination of UV light (365nm) even at a very low concentration (10μg/mL) of N,S-CDs aqueous solution can also clearly seen in the inset of Fig. 3. Furthermore, the FL intensity had no obvious decrease after 2h of UV light (365nm) illumination (Fig. S4), suggesting excellent photostability of the N,S-CDs. These implied that the N,S-CDs could be developed as a fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the fluorescence response of N,S-CDs to free chlorine
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 4 , the yellow N,S-CDs solution emits bright blue fluorescence under the excitation at 345nm, but the blue fluorescence was significantly quenched by the free chlorine (the inset of Fig. 4). Corresponding emission spectra show that the fluorescence intensity at 420nm has decreased about 80% in the presence of 100μM free chlorine. Meanwhile, change in the characteristic UV–vis absorption of N,S-CDs was observed, suggesting that the free chlorine quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs may be static quenching. To get further insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime experiments were used to investigate the excitation behavior of N,S-CDs in the presence and absence of free chlorine with various concentrations (Fig. S5). The results indicted that the fluorescence lifetimes are 9.79ns (absence of free chlorine), 9.76ns (0.01μM free chlorine), 9.75ns (0.1μM free chlorine), 9.73ns (1μM free chlorine), 9.74ns (10μM free chlorine) and 9.73ns (100μM free chlorine), respectively. Almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes indicated a static quenching mechanism, and confirmed that the free chlorine destroys the surface passivation layer of the N,S-CDs, leading to significant fluorescence quenching. At the same time, XPS was employed to investigate the state of sulfur and nitrogen after the addition of free chlorine. As shown in Figs. 5 and 6
, the spectrum of N(1s), S(2s), S(2p) decreased to vanish after interaction with free chlorine (5μM). These results confirmed that the free chlorine can destroy CS, CN and the surface passivation layer, Cl and N (or S) leaded to the generation of ground state complexes, thus the fluorescence of N,S-CDs was quenched.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the fluorescence sensing of free chlorine
ce:section.ce:para: To choose a suitable concentration of N,S-CDs for free chlorine detection, the relationship between fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs solution and concentration of N,S-CDs was investigated, and the results shown that the fluorescence intensity of system increase with the increase of N,S-CDs concentration in the range from 0.1 to 10μg/mL. However, the increasing tendency of fluorescence intensity slows down after the concentration of N,S-CDs is higher than 1.0μg/mL, and the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs will be insensitive to the addition of free chlorine. Therefore, the optimal N,S-CDs concentration is 1.0μg/mL.

It is well known that the pH of the solution is a parameter that not only affects the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs, but also plays an important role in the interactions between the N,S-CDs and target species [42]. To understand the effect of pH on the detection of free chlorine and obtain an optimum pH level for sensitive sensing of free chlorine, a parallel experiment for the system with and without free chlorine was completed. The pH was varied from 1.0 to 12.0 by using diluted HCl, 0.1M acetic–acetate buffer, 0.1M phosphate buffer, 0.1M Tris–HCl buffer and diluted NaOH solution. As shown in Fig. 7 , fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs is relatively low at a lower pH, which is unfavorable for developing a stable sensing method. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs reaches a maximum and keeps stable at pH higher than 4.0. On the other hand, pH value of solution also may affect the forms of free chlorine in water [43]. Free chlorine exists mainly as hypochloric acid (HClO) in neutral, weakly acidic and weakly alkaline solutions, and as hypochloriteanion (ClO−) in strong alkaline solutions (pH >9). The ClO− exhibits weaker oxidation capability than the HClO, which is a disadvantage in destroying the surface passivation of N,S-CDs and quenching the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Furthermore, the quenching effect of free chlorine on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs decreases with increasing pH value in neutral and weakly alkaline range (pH >7). Therefore, weakly acidic conditions (pH 3–6) are suitable, and the maximum quenching ability for free chlorine appeared at pH 5.0. Thus, pH 5.0 in 0.1M phosphate buffer was chosen for this sensing system.

To understand the response rate of the fluorescence signal of N,S-CDs to the free chlorine, the time-dependent fluorescence changes upon addition of 100μM free chlorine were monitored. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs at 420nm was quenched about 60% as soon as the free chlorine was added into the solution, quenched about 80% at 10min and kept stable during the following 1h. Thus, 10min was used as the optimal response time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances for free chlorine sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensitivity and the linear response range of the N,S-CDs fluorescent probe for free chlorine assay were investigated by analyzing different concentrations of free chlorine under the same experimental conditions. Fig. 8 shows the emission spectra of N,S-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of free chlorine. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs decreases with the increase of free chlorine concentration. Relative fluorescence intensity of system was linear to the concentration of free chlorine in the range from 0.01 to 100μM (see inset in Fig. 8). The linear regression was represented by the following equation:
F 0
− F
F 0
= 7.253 ×
10
3
C + 0.0252 (
R 2
= 0.9945 )
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of free chorine, respectively, and C is the concentration of free chlorine. The detection limit (defined as 3σ, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank) was 5nM. Over the past several years, various novel detection schemes have been developed for free chorine to improve the assay sensitivity. These methods include colorimetric chemosensor [44,45], CdSe–ZnS quantum dots fluorescent sensor [14], gold nanocomposite electrochemical sensor [46], graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fluorescent sensors [43,47], near-infrared fluorescence sensor [48], gold nanoclusters fluorescence sensor [49] and carbon nitride quantum dots (g-CNQDs) chemiluminescence sensor [50]. Table 1 summarizes the detection limits of some recent reported detection schemes for free chlorine assays. Considering none of these works was higher than proposed work in the detection limit, it is concluded that the proposed N,S-CDs fluorescent probe offers the best assay sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the fluorescent probe for free chlorine
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of N,S-CDs sensing platform, the effect of various ions on the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs was investigated. Different positive ions including Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Cr3+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Co2+ at a concentration of 0.1mM, and some negative ions such as F−, Cl−, Br−, S2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, CO3 2− and NO3 − at a concentration of 1mM were added into a 1.0μg/mL of N,S-CDs solution, and the fluorescence intensity of each solution was detected. As shown in Fig. 9 , HClO has furthest fluorescence quenching among the ions tested, whereas other ions have negligible effects on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs except Fe3+. And the interference from Fe3+ can be eliminated by triethanolamine. Obviously, this sensing system exhibits excellent selectivity for the detection of free chlorine.

To evaluate the feasibility of present method, the applicability of N,S-CDs fluorescent sensor for detection of free chlorine in tap water and swimming pool water were demonstrated. The contents of free chlorine in tap water and swimming pool water samples are summarized in Table 2 . The concentration of free residual chlorine in tap water sample was 10.85μM. The concentration of free chlorine in boiling tap water was lower than that in fresh tap water, indicating boiling water can decrease free chlorine concentration and reduce the harm to the human body. Recovery tests were performed to evaluate the accuracy of present method. A series of water samples spiked with free chlorine were analyzed, and detection results are also summarized in Table 2. As can be seen, the recoveries were in the range of 91.3–106.2% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 3.8% (n =5) for all of the samples analyzed.

In summary, N,S-CDs obtained from CA and l-cysteine has been demonstrated to be a promising fluorescence probe for high sensitive detection of free chlorine. The free chlorine can destroy the surface passivation layer of the N,S-CDs, forming ground state complexes without fluorescence leading to the fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs. This was utilized for the quantification of free chlorine. As fluorescence probe of free chlorine, N,S-CDs showed several advantages. First, it is a very simple, low-cost and green method. Second, the method is highly sensitive and selectivity, and had a wider linear range and lower detection limit. Third, the method is fast, and the response time is less than 10min. Therefore, the N,S-CDs could be used as fluorescence probe for simple, low-cost, and highly sensitive optical detection of free chlorine, and has been successfully applied to detect free chlorine in water. Which will envision having promising applications in water quality monitoring in the environmental analysis fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175030, 21327007), the BAGUI Scholar Program and the project of Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, Ministry of Education of China (CMEMR2013-KA952), the Scientific Research Project of Guangxi Higher Learning (No. 2013YB285).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.05.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mingyue Xue is an associate professor in Guilin Normal College and currently working toward the Ph.D. degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy of Guangxi Normal University, China. She received her master degree from Chongqing University in 2007. Her research interests are electrochemical sensor, functional nanomaterials and biological imaging.

Liangliang Zhang received both his M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University in 2009 and 2012, respectively. Now, he works in Guangxi Normal University and majors in chemical and biological sensing technology.

Mengbing Zou is currently working toward the bachelor degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy of Guangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests focus on functional nanomaterials and biological imaging.
Chuanqing Lan is currently working toward the master degree at the College of Chemistry and Pharmacy of Guangxi Normal University, China. His research interests focus on functional nanomaterials.

Zhihua Zhan is an associate professor in Guilin Normal College. He received his Ph.D. degree from Central South University in 2011. His research interests include electroanalysis and electrochemical sensor.

Shulin Zhao is a professor. He received his B.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University, and Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering from Sichuan University. Now, he works in Guangxi Normal University and majors in capillary electrophoresis, and chemical and biological sensing technology. He has published over 300 research papers.
